name: The EGC carries a small continuous leakage current normally
statement: Healthy appliances leak a little current to their bonded cases all the time, so the equipment grounding conductor normally carries a small continuous current.
correction: Healthy appliances should not leak any meaningful current to their bonded cases — that is what insulation is for. A few microamps of capacitive coupling exist on some equipment, but those are far below anything a breaker or GFCI reacts to. If continuous current is measured on an EGC in the field, it is almost always a wiring fault: a downstream N-G bond, a swapped neutral and ground, or compromised insulation. Designed behavior is zero EGC current under normal load — that is exactly what NEC 250.142 enforces by keeping the neutral and the EGC on separate buses downstream of the service disconnect.
